title: The Annexation Era
bullets: Japan formally annexed Korea on August 22, 1910, ending the 518-year Joseon dynasty and beginning 35 years of colonial rule under a Japanese Governor-General headquartered in a granite building deliberately built in front of Gyeongbokgung Palace to physically displace the old monarchy.

On March 1, 1919, thirty-three nationalists read a Declaration of Independence in Seoul; protests spread to roughly two million participants — a tenth of the Korean population — and Japanese police killed about 7,500 Koreans suppressing the uprising.

The March 1st Movement produced the Korean Provisional Government in Shanghai under Syngman Rhee, the institutional anchor of the independence movement for the next twenty-six years and the legal predecessor that the modern South Korean Constitution still recognizes.

After 1937, Japan launched a forced assimilation policy requiring Koreans to adopt Japanese names, worship at Shinto shrines, and stop speaking Korean in schools — generations of Koreans grew up partially illiterate in their own language.

The Imperial Japanese Army forced an estimated 50,000 to 200,000 women, the majority Korean, into military sexual slavery in 'comfort stations' across occupied Asia — a wartime mass-rape system that remains the most contested issue in Japan-Korea relations.
Japan conscripted hundreds of thousands of Korean men as wartime forced laborers in mines and factories like Hashima coal mine off Nagasaki, where survivors compared conditions to a prison camp.

The colonial regime built railways, ports, and chemical plants but extracted the surplus to Japan; per-capita Korean consumption stagnated through the colonial period despite headline economic growth, because the growth was extractive.
Japan's surrender on August 15, 1945 ended the colony — but two foreign powers had already decided that liberated Korea would be divided in half.
subtitle: thirty-five years as a Japanese colony

title: Liberation, Division, War
bullets: American officers Dean Rusk and Charles Bonesteel divided Korea at the 38th parallel in thirty minutes using a National Geographic map, intending it as a temporary occupation zone — the line hardened into one of the most heavily fortified borders on Earth.

The Republic of Korea was proclaimed in Seoul on August 15, 1948 with Syngman Rhee as president; the Democratic People's Republic of Korea was proclaimed in Pyongyang three weeks later under Kim Il-sung, a thirty-six-year-old former Manchurian guerrilla brought back in a Soviet uniform.

Before the war, the new South Korean state suppressed the Jeju Uprising and Yeosu-Suncheon Rebellion with violence that killed roughly 30,000 people on Jeju alone — about 10% of the island's population.

On June 25, 1950, North Korean forces crossed the 38th parallel in a coordinated invasion that took Seoul in three days and pushed UN and South Korean troops into a small enclave around Pusan within two months.

MacArthur's amphibious assault at Inchon on September 15, 1950 — through a 32-foot tidal range thought to make the port impossible — severed North Korean supply lines and recaptured Seoul within two weeks.

When UN forces approached the Yalu River in October 1950, roughly 300,000 Chinese troops crossed the border at night and drove the Americans back south of Seoul in the largest US military reverse since Bataan.

The Korean War armistice was signed on July 27, 1953 establishing the Demilitarized Zone — but South Korean President Syngman Rhee refused to authorize Korean participation, and no peace treaty was ever signed.

Roughly three million people died on a peninsula of thirty million — about 10% of the population — and US bomber commanders later estimated American air power had killed 20% of the North Korean population alone.
subtitle: the 38th parallel, MacArthur, Inchon, the Chinese intervention, and the unfinished war

title: Park Chung-hee and the Miracle on the Han
bullets: On May 16, 1961, Major General Park Chung-hee led a small group of paratroopers across the Han River and seized power; he ruled South Korea for the next eighteen years and engineered the most dramatic economic transformation any country has achieved in a comparable timeframe.

Park had served as a junior officer in the Manchukuo Imperial Army under the Japanese name Takagi Masao, and had narrowly survived a 1948 South Korean army purge of suspected Communist Party members by giving up the names of fellow Communist officers.

Park's developmentalist model — state-directed credit, picked national champions, export targets, suppressed labor — was the third wave of a Japanese template applied first to Japan itself, then to Manchukuo, then to Korea.
South Korean GDP grew at roughly 9% per year for three and a half decades; per-capita income rose from $80 in 1961 — lower than Sub-Saharan Africa — to $11,000 by 1996 and OECD membership.

The chaebol family conglomerates — Samsung, Hyundai, LG, Daewoo, Lotte, SK — emerged as state-favored champions; Samsung alone now accounts for roughly 20% of South Korean stock market capitalization.

South Korea deployed 320,000 troops to Vietnam alongside US forces — the second-largest foreign contingent — and American payments for Korean military service generated foreign currency that helped finance the early industrial miracle.

Park's 1972 Yushin Constitution gave him indefinite terms, the power to appoint a third of the National Assembly directly, and authority to rule by emergency decree — South Korea ceased to be a democracy in any meaningful sense and became an open dictatorship.
On October 26, 1979, KCIA director Kim Jae-gyu shot Park to death at a private dinner in a Seoul safehouse, ending eighteen years of personal rule; Kim was tried and hanged the following May.
subtitle: from $80 per capita to OECD membership in three decades

title: Two Koreas Diverge
bullets: In 1953 the northern half of the peninsula was the more economically developed half — colonial-era industrial infrastructure, hydroelectric dams, chemical plants — and Western Cold War analysts in the late 1950s expected the communist North to outperform the capitalist South.

By 2020 South Korean per-capita income was probably forty times North Korean, the most extreme political-economic divergence in modern history and one of the cleanest natural experiments anyone has ever run.

Kim Il-sung ruled North Korea from 1948 to 1994 — forty-six years, the longest personal autocracy of the twentieth century outside of Franco's Spain — and constructed a synthesis of Stalinism, Maoist mass mobilization, and Confucian filial piety called Juche.
North Korean military spending in the 1980s and 1990s reached an estimated 25–30% of GDP, the highest figure ever sustained by any country in peacetime.

The kwanliso prison camp system holds an estimated 80,000 to 120,000 political prisoners under conditions survivors describe as comparable to the Soviet Gulag at its worst, and applies the doctrine of three-generations-of-punishment to dissidents' families.
Major General Chun Doo-hwan seized power in two stages — the December 12, 1979 coup that took the army, and the May 17, 1980 expansion of martial law that crushed civilian opposition.

When citizens of Gwangju resisted Chun's martial law in May 1980, Special Forces troops beat and bayonet-stabbed protesters; the city armed itself, expelled the army, and was self-governing for six days before the army retook it on May 27 with hundreds of civilian deaths.

Gwangju became the moral foundation of South Korean democracy — Chun and his successor Roh Tae-woo were both convicted of treason for the December coup and the Gwangju massacre in 1996, the only Korean ex-presidents convicted of treason for taking power.
subtitle: Park's Yushin, Kim Il-sung's Juche, Gwangju, and how the peninsula split

title: Democratization
bullets: In January 1987, police interrogators at the Namyeong-dong center in Seoul waterboarded 22-year-old Seoul National University student Park Jong-chul to death and tried to claim he had collapsed of natural causes; a courageous medical examiner refused to sign off and a Catholic priest broke the cover-up.

The June Democracy Movement of 1987 — millions of students and white-collar middle-class Koreans in the streets, plus the iconic photograph of Yonsei student Lee Han-yeol bleeding from a tear-gas canister — forced Roh Tae-woo to capitulate on June 29 and accept direct presidential elections.

The opposition split between Kim Young-sam and Kim Dae-jung handed the December 1987 election to former coup leader Roh Tae-woo with 36.6% of the vote — a bitter outcome that South Korean political memory has not entirely forgiven.

The 1988 Seoul Olympics, watched by an estimated three billion people, were South Korea's coming-out party and the international showcase for the political opening that had been forced through the year before.
Kim Young-sam's Civilian Government tried Chun Doo-hwan and Roh Tae-woo for treason and corruption in 1996 and convicted both — the only Korean ex-presidents convicted of treason for taking power.

The 1997 IMF crisis required a $58 billion bailout, the largest in IMF history at the time, and broke the chaebol's perceived invincibility; the Korean economy contracted 5.5% in 1998, unemployment quintupled, and roughly 3.5 million Koreans donated 227 tons of personal gold jewelry to help service the foreign debt.

Kim Dae-jung — kidnapped by the KCIA in 1973, sentenced to death in 1980 — won the December 1997 presidential election as the first Korean president elected from the opposition, the first peaceful transfer of power between rival parties.
Kim Dae-jung met Kim Jong-il in Pyongyang in June 2000 — the first inter-Korean summit since the war — launching the Sunshine Policy of engagement; he won the Nobel Peace Prize that year.
subtitle: a torture death, the June Movement, the IMF crisis, and Kim Dae-jung's Sunshine Policy

title: K-Wave and Globalization
bullets: PSY's 'Gangnam Style' became the first YouTube video to reach one billion views in December 2012; President Obama danced to it at the White House and the Saudi religious police banned it.

The Korean Wave — Hallyu — was named by Chinese journalists in the late 1990s after Korean dramas became surprise hits in mainland China; the prototype was Winter Sonata in 2002, which made its lead actor Bae Yong-joon one of the highest-paid foreign celebrities in Japan.

K-pop's manufactured-idol-group model — trainees recruited as young as eleven, signed to multi-year contracts, dorm-housed, drilled in dance and multi-language fluency — produced groups like BTS, who topped the Billboard 200 album chart five times, the first Korean act in any genre to do so.

Bong Joon-ho's Parasite won Best Picture, Best Director, Best Original Screenplay, and Best International Feature at the 2020 Oscars — the first non-English-language film to win Best Picture in the Academy Awards' 92-year history.

Squid Game became Netflix's most-watched show ever within four weeks of its September 2021 release, reaching 142 million viewing households and cementing Korean content's place at the center of global streaming.

The Korean Wave was not a spontaneous cultural movement; the Korean state has subsidized cultural exports through the Korea Creative Content Agency since 2009 with budgets in the hundreds of millions of dollars.

Hallyu's reach has been deepest in markets where the Korean government has invested most heavily in language and cultural promotion — the model is closer to Korean industrial export policy than to Hollywood.

The biggest Korean creative successes of the 2020s have been streaming productions where Korean directors work with Western producers and budgets, complicating the original soft-power industrial-policy logic.
subtitle: from Winter Sonata to Squid Game — and the policy machine that made it happen

title: The North Korean Question
bullets: The Arduous March famine of 1994–98 killed an estimated 600,000 to 2.5 million people in a country of about 22 million — at the upper bound, more than 10% of the population — caused by Soviet collapse, Chinese aid reductions, collective-farm collapse, and a regime that prioritized army and Pyongyang loyalists over the broader population.
Survivor accounts describe whole village populations dying, organized markets in human meat in some northeastern provinces, and hospitals operating without medicine, food, or electricity.

The famine produced the informal jangmadang market system, which is now the principal way most North Koreans get food, clothing, and household goods — North Korea is no longer a fully totalitarian society but a totalitarian regime presiding over a partially marketized population.
North Korea conducted six nuclear tests between 2006 and 2017; the 2017 thermonuclear test had an estimated yield over 100 kilotons, roughly six times Hiroshima.

The strategic logic of the North Korean nuclear program is straightforward — the regime watched Saddam Hussein deposed without nuclear weapons and Muammar Gaddafi overthrown after surrendering his program, and concluded weapons were the only reliable guarantor of regime survival.

Kim Il-sung died of a heart attack on July 8, 1994; his son Kim Jong-il took over for seventeen years, then his grandson Kim Jong-un took over in 2011 and consolidated power by executing his uncle Jang Song-thaek in 2013 and assassinating his half-brother Kim Jong-nam by VX nerve agent at Kuala Lumpur airport in 2017.

Donald Trump met Kim Jong-un in Singapore on June 12, 2018 — the first meeting between sitting American and North Korean leaders — but the follow-up Hanoi summit in February 2019 collapsed without an agreement and the program continued.

The world has, in practice, accepted that North Korea has nuclear weapons; the strategic conversation has shifted from denuclearization to managing the consequences, and South Korean public-opinion polling now shows rising support for an indigenous nuclear program.
subtitle: the famine, the bombs, the dynasty, and why the regime keeps not collapsing

title: The Twenty-First Century
bullets: Samsung Electronics is the largest memory chip maker in the world, and combined with SK Hynix, South Korea controls roughly 60% of global DRAM production and over 50% of global NAND flash production.

The top ten chaebol account for roughly 80% of South Korean GDP and Samsung alone for about 20% of stock market capitalization — a level of corporate concentration without parallel in any other major economy.

South Korea's total fertility rate fell to 0.72 in 2023 — the lowest of any country ever recorded in the modern era, less than half the population-replacement level — driven by Seoul housing costs, work-hour culture, and the gendered structure of childcare.

Park Geun-hye, daughter of Park Chung-hee and Korea's first female president, was impeached in December 2016 over the Choi Soon-sil corruption scandal and removed by the Constitutional Court in March 2017 with millions of citizens holding weekly Candlelight Protests in central Seoul.

On the night of October 29, 2022, a Halloween crowd surge in a narrow alley in Seoul's Itaewon district killed 159 people, mostly young Koreans in their twenties, sharpening debates over public-safety governance.

On December 3, 2024, President Yoon Suk-yeol declared martial law in a televised speech, deploying soldiers to the National Assembly building in central Seoul — the first martial law declaration since Chun Doo-hwan in 1980.

The National Assembly convened an emergency session at one in the morning, voted 190-0 to overturn the declaration, and Yoon withdrew it about six hours after issuing it; he was impeached on December 14.

If Joseon's failure was its inability to modernize when the world demanded it, the Republic of Korea's challenge is the inverse — it has modernized brilliantly and now has to figure out how to govern itself sustainably in the world that modernization made.
subtitle: Samsung, the demographic crisis, the impeachments, and a martial-law moment
